This variation requires significantly less bulking agent (average of 0.4 versus 0.7 mL), the procedure is simple to perform, and the reported success rate for resolution of VUR is improved compared to the standard STING surgery, up to 91%. Following the initial learning period, the HIT injection method appears to be superior to the STING or modified STING procedures, with success rates of 89% versus 71%. However, some studies have shown no significant difference between the 2 techniques. The surgeon's experience with endoscopic injections is an independent predictor of the final success rate. The initial 110 cases show a clear learning curve, with gradual improvement until the success rate stabilizes. Proficiency is typically achieved after at least 20 cases. Despite being generally regarded as a straightforward procedure, technical expertise and experience significantly influence outcomes, especially in complex cases like higher-grade VUR and ureteral duplications. Increasing the amount of bulking material typically enhances the final success rate but also leads to higher costs.
Document [2] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery) HIT method: The HIT method, introduced by Kirsch in 2004, utilizes hydrostatic pressure to distend the intramural ureteral lumen, facilitating improved visualization. The injection needle is placed within the lumen of the intramural ureter at the 6 o'clock position before traversing the ureter's posterior wall into the submucosal space, where the bulking agent is injected to form a volcano-shaped mound. This technique allows for more optimal placement of the bulking agent due to enhanced needle accuracy, resulting in an augmented antireflux effect within the intramural ureter, extending beyond primarily at the orifice.

Document [10] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux) Dextranomere-hyaluronic acid copolymer: Introduced in 2002, the dextranomere-hyaluronic acid copolymer is a viscous gel containing dextranomere microspheres in a hyaluronic acid carrier base. Other features are listed below. The microspheres have an intermediate size (80-250 μm in diameter). The material is non-biodegradable and appears immunologically inert. This material causes minimal scarring and has no apparent malignancy-promoting properties. A minimal histological reaction is not apparent to the injected material. The copolymer is less likely to cause ureteral obstruction over time. The typical injected volume is about 1 mL (ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 mL) and is slightly higher on average than that of polyacrylate polyalcohol.
Document [11] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux) Bulking agents currently used for endoscopic antireflux surgery include dextranomer-hyaluronic acid gel and polyacrylate-polyalcohol copolymer, which have been used as injectable bulking agents for over a decade, especially for lower-grade VUR, yielding similar outcomes. Document [12] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Predictors of Successful Endoscopic Treatment) Endoscopic periureteral bulking therapy has shown better outcomes under various conditions and circumstances. The grade of VUR significantly influences the outcome and is the most crucial factor in predicting successful endoscopic surgery, according to one meta-analysis. Reported success rates in this analysis were as follows: Grade I: 89% Grade II: 83% Grade III: 71% Grade IV: 59% Grade V: 62% A recent study involving over 850 cases of VUR treated endoscopically demonstrated resolution in 70.4% of grade IV cases and 61.9% of grade V cases after a single periureteral injection procedure. Recurrent or residual reflux was resolved in an additional 20.1% after a second injection and 10.4% more after a third injection.
Document [13] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery) Endoscopic surgical treatment of VUR is considered the preferred initial surgical approach. This approach is minimally invasive, requires minimal anesthesia, and provides an immediate cure for most patients. In addition, it avoids the complications of long-term antibiotic prophylaxis, does not rely on strict patient compliance, has shown durable resolution of grade IV and V reflux, and can be easily repeated if needed. Document [14] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery) STING technique: The STING procedure, initially reported in 1981 and later in 1984, is one of the more commonly used endoscopic treatments for VUR. As first described, polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) was used as the initial bulking agent, which has now been replaced with newer materials. STING utilizes endoscopic insertion of the injection needle submucosally into the bladder just below (2-3 mm) the ureteral orifice at the 6 o'clock position. As originally described, the needle is advanced within the submucosa for 2 to 3 mm, and the bulking material is injected to create a crescentric ureteral orifice.
Document [15] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Predictors of Successful Endoscopic Treatment) A lower number of previous endoscopic surgical procedures is associated with a higher overall success rate. Successful outcomes correlate with proper mound configuration and appearance. The presence of bowel or bladder dysfunction negatively predicts VUR control after endoscopic surgery. Untreated abnormal bladder function significantly increases VUR recurrence. Preoperative bladder dysfunction is not a contraindication to endoscopic surgery but should be addressed before surgical treatment. Endoscopic treatment is more likely to fail in patients aged younger than 1 and those with existing renal scarring. Endoscopic treatment success rates are enhanced by administering larger volumes of periureteral bulking agents and employing multiple injection sites, particularly at high-volume centers of excellence.
Document [16] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux) Polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer: Introduced in 2010, polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer is a synthetic, non-biodegradable periureteral bulking agent used for endoscopic periureteral injections to treat VUR. The copolymer features a relatively large microsphere size of 320 μm, minimizing particle migration. The material's non-biodegradability ensures greater stability and long-term efficacy. No apparent malignancy-promoting effect is observed. The material tends to form a fibrous capsule with significant scarring. Severe inflammation, scarring, and fibrosis typically develop at the injection site, potentially leading to ureteral obstruction long after administration. This outcome is attributed to a persistent foreign body reaction, the use of alcohol polymers, and the relatively large microsphere size. The treatment may result in ureteral obstruction over time, necessitating long-term follow-up. No evidence suggests a malignancy-promoting effect.

Document [30] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Complications -- Complications of Surgical Procedures for Vesicoureteric Reflux) Complications following endoscopic VUR procedures are rare. Document [31] (Title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Standard Non-Endoscopic Ureteral Reimplantation Antireflux Surgeries) The bladder is evacuated of saline and inflated with gas to a pressure of no greater than 7 mm Hg. Bilateral ureteral stenting is performed. Ureters are dissected circumferentially to mobilize a segment of no less than 4 cm or enough length to reach the contralateral ostium. In circumstances of bilateral VUR, submucosal tunneling should be prepared. To reduce tension on the created neo-ureteral orifice, the mobilized ureter is sutured to the bladder entrance. Mucosal defects are closed upon completion of the procedure.

Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 451) Ureteral catheterization is a core category of procedures performed on the ureter, encompassing a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided techniques. Specific interventions that are classified as ureteral catheterization include cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These procedures are also linked to “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS,” which is recognized as a sub‑type of ureteral catheterization and is described in Arabic as عملية في الحالب. Ureteral catheterization itself is a subtype of the broader “Procedures on Ureter” hierarchy, and it is further situated within the larger domain of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction. Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1609) Procedures on Ureter is a broad category that encompasses specific ureteral interventions such as transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction. This hierarchical relationship links the English‑language procedural descriptions with their Arabic counterpart, clarifying that both the transurethral excision/drainage/removal technique and ureteral injections and implantations are subtypes of the general “procedure in the ureter.”

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402) Ureteroplication is a surgical procedure that is performed directly on the ureter, targeting the ureter itself to correct or modify its structure for therapeutic purposes.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2000) Renal endoscopy through established nephrostomy or pyelostomy with ureteral catheterization and dilation of ureter is a urological intervention that directly involves the ureter, as the procedure utilizes the ureter for catheter placement and dilation after accessing the kidney via a nephrostomy or pyelostomy.

Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 783) Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures encompass a range of endoscopic interventions on the lower urinary tract, and within this group the specific procedure “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction” is identified as a child technique. This transurethral excision, drainage, or removal procedure is classified as a type of “Procedures on Ureter,” indicating that it directly addresses pathology of the ureter and is therefore closely related to the ureter itself. Both “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction” and “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” share a common hierarchical parent denoted by the Arabic term “عملية في الحالب” (procedure in the ureter), situating them under a broader category of ureteral interventions. In addition, the procedure “Ureteroplication” is explicitly linked to the ureter through the relationship “direct_procedure_site_of,” highlighting it as a distinct surgical technique aimed at correcting ureteral abnormalities. Collectively, these interrelated procedures—Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures, Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction, Procedures on Ureter, URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, and Ureteroplication—form an integrated framework for diagnosing, relieving, and managing urinary obstruction and other ureteral conditions, emphasizing the importance of precise procedural classification and anatomical targeting in patient care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 223) The community describes a hierarchical framework of ureteral and renal pelvic procedures. Ureteral catheterization is a central procedural category that encompasses endoscopic and fluoroscopic techniques such as cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These interventions are classified under the broader groups “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures” and “Procedures on ureter,” and they include the child procedure “Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction.” The Arabic‑named parent procedure عملية في الحالب (procedure in the ureter) further contains sub‑procedures such as URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS. Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1310) Cystoscopic catheterization of ureter is classified as a type of ureteral catheterization. The procedure URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is also placed under the broader category of ureteral catheterization and is additionally linked to the Arabic‑language category عملية في الحالب. Ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, as well as both bilateral and unilateral Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of upper urinary tract, are primarily derived from the concept of ureteral catheterization, indicating that they are specialized extensions of this core procedure. Moreover, the bilateral and unilateral Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of upper urinary tract are also associated with the category Other x-ray of the urinary system, reflecting that fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract is considered an ancillary radiographic modality within urinary system imaging. Together, these relationships illustrate how various endoscopic and catheter‑based interventions on the ureter are organized under the umbrella of ureteral catheterization and related imaging categories, supporting a structured approach to procedural classification and documentation in urologic care.
